coredata.dc:title: Stable and active low-Pt electrocatalysts using defective carbon support assembled from graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Junjie
Zhao, Zongtao
Zhang, Bin
Xu, Jiansheng
Lin, Chao
Li, Xiaopeng
Gu, Xiongyi
coredata.dc:description: Reducing precious metal loading is of paramount importance in electrocatalysis. Here, we propose an efficient strategy to synthesize low-Pt electrocatalysts (3.9 wt%) with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance comparable to that of a state-of-the-art 20% Pt/C electrocatalyst. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as building blocks for carbon support. Cobalt catalyzed the GQDs assembly into porous carbon nanosheets with plenty of defects and high N doping. Pt was deposited on the support via in-situ electrochemical cycling. The combination of low Pt nanoparticles loading and defective carbon remarkably enhanced the HER activity in both acid (overpotential = 84 mV at 100 mA cm−2, Tafel slope of 45 mV dec−1) and alkaline electrolytes (overpotential = 300 mV at 100 mA−2, Tafel slope of 152 mV dec−1) while showing excellent stability and durability for 45 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Defective carbon support
Graphene quantum dots
Low-Pt electrocatalysts
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HER performance of the CV-activated samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM and TEM (c, d) images of Co/QNC-NH3.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra and XPS N 1s spectra of (c) Co/GO-NH3, (d) QNC-NH3, and (e) Co/QNC-NH3. (f) Quantitatively comparison of different N species distribution for all of the samples.

CV curves for (a) QNC-NH3 and (b) Co/QNC-NH3 with 1500 cycles and 5000 cycles to obtain the Pt/QNC-NH3 and Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 samples, respectively, with a sweep rate of 100 mV s−1. (c, d) TEM images of Co/QNC-NH3 catalysts after the activation process. (e) Cdl plots obtained from CV cures with scan rate in the range of 1 to 10 mV s−1 for Co/QNC-NH3 and Co/GO-NH3 before and after the activation process. (f) XPS Pt 4f spectrum of Co/QNC-NH3 after CV activation.

(a) Polarization curves and (b) Tafel plots for Pt/QNC-NH3, Pt-Co/QNC-NH3, Co/GO-NH3 and 20% Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (c) Mass specific activity of Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 and 20% Pt/C at various overpotentials (vs RHE). (d) Comparison of HER performance Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 with reported Pt-based or noble-metal-free catalysts. (e) Galvanostatic measurement at an overpotential of 50 mV (vs RHE) for Pt/QNC-NH3 and Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 in 0.5 M H2SO4. (f) Polarization curves for Pt/QNC-NH3, Pt-Co/QNC-NH3, Co/GO-NH3, and 20% Pt/C in a 1 M KOH electrolyte. All polarization curves were collected at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1.

Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure used herein to prepare the Co/QNC-NH3 catalysts. The homogeneously doped N atoms on the surface of defective carbon support promote the deposition of Pt, which is the key for the formation of the highly active Pt nanoparticles during multi-cycle activation.

head.ce:title: Stable and active low-Pt electrocatalysts using defective carbon support assembled from graphene quantum dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing precious metal loading is of paramount importance in electrocatalysis. Here, we propose an efficient strategy to synthesize low-Pt electrocatalysts (3.9 wt%) with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance comparable to that of a state-of-the-art 20% Pt/C electrocatalyst. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as building blocks for carbon support. Cobalt catalyzed the GQDs assembly into porous carbon nanosheets with plenty of defects and high N doping. Pt was deposited on the support via in-situ electrochemical cycling. The combination of low Pt nanoparticles loading and defective carbon remarkably enhanced the HER activity in both acid (overpotential = 84 mV at 100 mA cm−2, Tafel slope of 45 mV dec−1) and alkaline electrolytes (overpotential = 300 mV at 100 mA−2, Tafel slope of 152 mV dec−1) while showing excellent stability and durability for 45 h.

ce:section.ce:para: Although our global economy is currently based on fossil fuels energy, renewable and reliable clean energy will inevitably become the new fulcrum for global economic progress in the near future [1]. Molecular hydrogen (H2) possesses the highest mass-energy density among the clean energy vectors, being one of the resources for energy storage and conversion with high potential [2–4]. Unfortunately, most hydrogen production is still based on steam-reformed methane, resulting in large amounts of CO2 as a byproduct [5]. Considering environmental hazards and healthiness, water splitting has been recognized as the most promising alternative to the current fossil fuel-based process for hydrogen production [6–8].

Nowadays, the most effective hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) electrocatalysts are Pt-based catalysts, although this precious metal is costly and scarce [9–11]. In this sense, significant efforts have been dedicated to designing HER catalysts based on non-noble metals including earth-abundant metal-based materials and metal-free catalysts [12–14]. Materials such as MoS2 [15], FeP [16], and P-doped Co3O4 [17] have shown high HER electrocatalytic activities, however, their performance is still insufficient for commercial applications. To balance the cost of H2 production and HER activities, it is necessary to minimize the mass loading of Pt in HER catalysts and enhance the Pt coefficient of utilization. For instance, Liu et al. reported that Pt-decorated defect-rich SnS2 nanosheets exhibited greatly improved HER activity despite having very low Pt loadings [18]. By using atomic layer deposition (ALD), Cheng et al. [10] prepared single platinum atoms and clusters supported on N-doped graphene nanosheets. These catalysts showed high Pt utilization and significantly enhanced electrocatalytic activity. In recent years, some groups have demonstrated that Pt nanoparticles supported N-doped carbon frameworks exhibit high effective Pt utilization and excellent HER performance in virtue of the modification of the Pt electronic structure by N species [19,20]. Recent reports showed that the HER performance of the working electrode can be improved by using Pt wire as a counter electrode in an acid electrolyte, due to the deposit of Pt atoms on the surface of the working electrode [21–23]. Moreover, as Gottlieb et. al [21] demonstrated, pyridinic N atoms can serve as active sites for Pt coordination and deposition during electrochemical activation. However, pyridinic N-rich carbon-based materials decorate with ultralow Pt loading via electrochemical activation strategy for HER have been scarcely reported. Transition metals such as Ni and Co can also effectively improve the electrochemical surface area, the defect density of carbon-based materials, potentially enhancing the Pt transfer behavior during the activation process [24–26].

Herein, we report an attractive strategy to prepare low-loading Pt electrocatalysts. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as the basic building blocks to prepare the carbon support. Cobalt was used to catalyze the formation of N-doped graphitic carbon under a reducing ammonia environment, resulting in the assembly of GQDs into porous carbon support after pyrolysis. Meanwhile, GQDs have been known to possess abundant oxygenated functional groups. Cobalt also catalyzed etching of the in-situ formed carbon nanosheets with the excess supply of oxygen species from GQDs, creating additional pores and defects. Compared with a control sample prepared with graphene oxide (GO) as the carbon building block, the GQDs-derived carbon support showed higher porosity and defectiveness. Once Pt was loaded by electrochemical cycling, the strong interaction between Pt and the GQDs-derived carbon support greatly enhanced the HER activity in both acid (overpotential = 84 mV at 100 mA cm−2, Tafel slope of 45 mV dec−1) and alkaline electrolytes (overpotential = 300 mV at 100 mA−2, Tafel slope of 152 mV dec−1). The material also showed excellent stability and durability for 45 h, comparable to that of a state-of-the-art 20% Pt/C catalyst.

Firstly, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized following previous reports [27]. Then, 2 mL of a 3 mmol mL−1 Co(NO3)2 solution and 5 mL of the 4 mg mL−1 as-prepared N-GQDs solution were added in a capped bottle. After 15 min of vigorous stirring, the mixture was freeze-dried for at least 24 h. The as-obtained brown powder was placed in a standard furnace. After exchanging the atmosphere with Ar three times, the temperature was ramped at 5 °C min−1 to 650 °C and maintained for 2 h while flowing an Ar and NH3 mixture (VAr: V NH3 = 3:1). A black powder noted as Co/QNC-NH3 was obtained after the furnace was cooled to room temperature under Ar-atmosphere protection. A QNC-NH3 sample was prepared following the same treatment and not adding Co(NO3)2 in the precursor solution. A QNC-Ar sample was prepared following the same treatment with QNC-NH3 except annealed under Ar atmosphere. A Co/GO-NH3 material was fabricated by using as a precursor solution a mixture of 2 mL of 3 mmol mL−1 Co(NO3)2 solution with 5 mL of a 4 mg mL−1 GO solution.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL-6700F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Titan Krios). The crystal structure of the samples was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer) with a Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG SCIENTA, R 3000) was used to characterize the state of the elements in all the samples before and after activation. Moreover, Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw, RM 1000) obtained under a 512 nm laser was used to determine the graphitic degrees of the carbon materials. Inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to determine the Pt mass loading of the sample after CV activation. The working electrode with catalyst mass loading of 1 mg was activated for 5000 cycles. The activated electrode was digested by a Teflon pressure vessel at 180 °C for 4 h by using aqua regia as solvent. The solution was transferred into a volumetric flask and filled to the mark with 4% HCl solution before the ICP-OES measurement.

The electrochemical testing was carried out on a three-electrode cell containing Pt wires, Ag/AgCl (3.5 M), and a carbon paper with catalysts loading of 1 mg cm−2 as the counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. To prepare the catalyst-supported carbon paper electrode, 5 mg catalyst powder was dispersed in a 1 mL mixture of water/isopropanol (3:1, v/v) solution, then 20 μL 5 wt% Nafion solution (Sigma-Aldrich) was added. The homogeneous ink solution was obtained after ultrasonication for more than 1 h. and then 200 μL of catalyst ink was carefully drop-cast on 1 cm × 1 cm carbon paper (JNT20) under heating conditions. All tests were carried out in a 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte (pH = 0.3). Before the HER performance testing, the working electrodes were activated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) over a − 0.3–0.3 V vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) range at a sweep rate of 100 mV s−1 until stable catalytic current density was obtained. The polarization curves were recorded over a potential range of 0 to − 0.6 V (vs RHE) at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1. All the potential were referred to the RHE with a 100% iR compensation by the following calculation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.205 + 0.059 ×pH-iR. The uncompensated solution resistances were obtained by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz, where R is determined to be 1.8 Ω.

Scheme 1 shows the synthetic method used to prepare the Co/QNC-NH3 composites. Firstly, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were well-dispersed in a Co(NO3)2 solution by sonication. Then, the precursor solution was freeze-dried for at least 24 h at − 48 °C to obtain a completely dried brown powder. After heating at 650 °C under NH3, the Co/QNC-NH3 black powder was successfully prepared. By changing the precursor, two control samples of N-GQDs derived N-doped carbon (QNC-NH3) and Co supported graphene oxide (Co/GO-NH3) were also prepared by the same method. The morphology of these three samples was examined by SEM and TEM. SEM images shown in Fig. 1a and b revealed that Co/QNC-NH3 has a highly porous structure, unlike QNC-NH3 and Co/GO-NH3 having bulk and nanosheet structures, respectively (Fig. S1(a, c)). According to the TEM images of Co/QNC-NH3 (Fig. 1(c, d)), cobalt nanoparticles of ca. 10–50 nm were encapsulated within the porous carbon frameworks surrounding with large amounts of similar size spheroidal pores. Conversely, in the case of Co/GO-NH3, small size cobalt nanoparticles were uniformly distributed on the surface of GO without any visible defects (Fig. S1(d)). And QNC-NH3 showed no porous structure at all (Fig. S1(b)). Thus, CoOx species produced by Co(NO3)2 decomposition likely corroded amorphous carbon to create more defects and pores during the carbonization process, which hardly occurs with highly stable graphitic carbon.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), Co/QNC-NH3 showed XRD peaks at 43.8, 51, and 75.2°, which were assigned to the (111), (200), and (220) reflections of cobalt (PDF# 15–0806). No significant peaks were observed for Co/GO-NH3 due to the low Co loading and small particle size, in line with TEM results. Raman peaks at ca. 1359 and 1592 cm−1 were assigned to disordered carbon atoms (D band) and sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon atoms (G band). The area integration ratio (ID/IG) of Co/GO-NH3, Co/QNC-NH3, and QNC-NH3 were 2.38, 2.88, and 2.84, respectively, revealing a high concentration of defects in the carbon matrix after pyrolysis for ammonia-prepared samples. Especially, the significantly larger ID/IG ratio of Co/QNC-NH3 and QNC-NH3 samples suggested that quantum dots derived porous carbon frameworks facilitated the creation of numerous defect sites by N-doping. High-resolution XPS spectra provided more information about the bonding configuration of the N species. As shown in Fig. 2(c–f) and S2, the N 1 s spectra were deconvoluted into four peaks attributed to pyridinic-N (ca. 398.5 eV), pyrrolic-N (ca. 400.2 eV), graphitic-N (ca. 401.2 eV), and oxide-N (~403.0 eV) species [19]. Importantly, considering the high content of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N species in the Co/QNC-NH3 and QNC-NH3 samples, excellent HER performance can be anticipated for these samples.

CV curves in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte were used as an activation process, in which Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively, while the samples supported on carbon paper served as working electrodes and were measured with an applied potential range of − 0.3–0.3 V vs. RHE at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The electrochemical behavior of the samples is shown in Fig. 3(a, b), and S3. For QNC-Ar and Co/GO-NH3, the activation process did not improve the HER catalytic activities, which even decreased in some cases (Fig. S3). Notably, during the CV activation process, the catalytic current densities of QNC-NH3 and Co/QNC-NH3 gradually increased as the number of CV scans increased, reaching the maximum efficiency after 1200 and 5000 cycles, respectively. As the number of scan cycles increased, the redox reactions taking place on the N-doped carbon frameworks can result in morphology changes. Furthermore, Pt loading (3.9 wt%) was measured with high precision by ICP-OES for activated-Co/QNC-NH3. As expected, anodic dissolution of Pt from the Pt counter electrode took place on the working electrode after certain CV cycles. TEM observations were performed to examine the effect of the CV activation process on the morphology of Co/QNC-NH3. Unlike the pristine sample (Fig. 1(c, d)), large-size Co nanoparticles almost disappeared after the activation, resulting in a more porous structure (Fig. 3(c, d)). It is worth mentioning that the existence of a large amount of porous increased the exposure of active sites, thereby increasing the HER activity. The double-layer capacitance of the Co/QNC-NH3 increased from 0.43 to 176 mF cm−2 after the CV tests, in line with TEM results and confirming the increased electrochemical surface area (ECSA) (Fig. 3(e) and S4). The higher ECSA for CV activated-Co/QNC-NH3 revealed a higher number of active sites and enhanced anion accessibility, which contributed to the high HER activity of the sample. To investigate the state of Pt, XPS was performed again for the CV activated-Co/QNC-NH3 material. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the Pt characteristic spectrum was deconvoluted into 71.6 eV (4f7/2) and 77.5 eV (4f5/2) bands ascribed to metal Pt, and into 73.6 eV and 76.8 eV bands attributed to Pt2+ [28]. The fitted spectra of Pt 4f shifted to higher energies compared to that of bulk Pt, suggesting a strong interaction between Pt and the support due to the presence of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N species [29,30]. To investigate the role of the current collector during the CV activation process, QNC-NH3 was supported on a glassy carbon electrode with mass loading of 0.2 mg cm−2 following the multi-CV activation process. The greatly enhanced HER performance as shown in Fig. S5 suggest that the nature of catalyst are key to the deposition of Pt species. Therefore, the above results suggested that CV can be used as an effective in-situ activation strategy to obtain Pt decorated defects over enriched N-doped carbon with improved HER activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HER performance of the CV-activated samples
ce:section.ce:para: HER catalytic activities were carefully measured on a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell under an N2 atmosphere. Fig. 4(a) shows the electrocatalytic activities of all the CV-activated samples with mass loading density similar to that of 20% Pt/C (1 mg cm−2) in 0.5 M H2SO4 at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The polarization curves of both the Pt-decorated Co/QNC-NH3 (Pt-Co/QNC-NH3) and QNC-NH3 (Pt/QNC-NH3) revealed significantly low overpotentials of 84 and 102 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, respectively. Detailed kinetic mechanism of the HER processes was obtained from the Tafel plots. The H2 generation processes in acidic electrolytes consist of two steps, a first hydrogen atom adsorption step achieved through Volmer reaction and a second hydrogen desorption step via the Heyrovsky or Tafel reaction. Tafel slopes of 30 mV s−1, 40 mV s−1, and 120 mV s−1 are normally observed for Tafel-, Heyrovsky-, or Volmer-limited processes, respectively [31]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), small Tafel slopes of 45 and 40 mV dec−1 were observed for Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 and Pt/QNC-NH3 samples, respectively. These slopes were comparable to that of 20%Pt/C, revealing a Volmer–Heyrovsky mechanism. Meanwhile, the exchange current density of Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 and Co/QNC-NH3 samples were determined to be 1.1 and 1.2 mA cm−2, respectively (Table S1). Fig. 4(c) shows the calculated mass-specific activities based on Pt mass loadings at various potentials. Notably, Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 showed a mass-specific activity 6.7 times greater than that of commercial 20% Pt/C (0.61 A mgPt −1). The HER performance of the samples prepared herein was higher than those of most reported HER catalysts in acidic media (Fig. 4(d)) [32–38]. According to the EDS mapping (Figs. S6 and S7) and elemental analysis results (Table S2 and S3), the in-situ Pt deposition property of graphene quantum dots derived electrocatalysts are highly related to the high concentration of N species, and the enhanced HER performance might be attributed to the high dispersion of Pt and strong interaction between Pt and N related defects after CV activation [29,30].

Stability was another significant standard to appraise advanced electrocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the chronoamperometric curves of Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 and Pt/QNC-NH3 under overpotential of 50 mV shows a slight cathodic current loss even after 45 h operation, revealing their excellent stability. When measured in alkaline media (1 M KOH), Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 also showed higher activity than the activated QNC-Ar, Co/GO-NH3, and Pt/QNC-NH3 samples, which is close to that of 20% Pt/C with the same mass loading (Fig. 4(f)). These results indicated that Pt-Co/QNC-NH3 was highly active in both acid and alkaline electrolytes, which is distinguished with some metal sulfide (e.g., MoS2) and metal phosphide-based catalysts (e.g., Ni2P) that stable in acid, but degradation in alkaline electrolyte [39,40].

Cobalt-supported N-doped porous carbon frameworks having very small amounts of Pt can function as highly active and robust HER catalysts in both acid and base media. This excellent catalytic performance, with maximum Pt utilization and low-cost preparation, makes this catalyst a promising candidate to replace 20% Pt/C for hydrogen evolution applications. In addition, the electrochemical CV activation process can be an effective in-situ metal decorated strategy to synthesize other efficient electrocatalysts with broader applicability.

Junjie Cheng: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zongtao Zhao: Investigation, Resources. Bin Zhang: Resources. Jiansheng Xu: Resources. Chao Lin: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Xiaopeng Li: Project administration, Supervision. Xiongyi Gu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21972163), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, the DHU Distinguished Young Professor Program, the Development Fund for Shanghai Talents, and the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.163441.
